turns.content: been reading about the mingei movement and i need to talk about it with someone before my brain explodes. do you know much about it

I know the basics but I'd love to hear what's got you so excited about it! Mingei — the folk craft movement started by Yanagi Soetsu in the 1920s, right? Celebrating the beauty of everyday handmade objects rather than fine art. What specifically has you deep in the rabbit hole?

yes okay so yanagi soetsu is honestly one of the most underrated thinkers in design history. like the whole premise is that beauty comes from anonymous craftsmen making functional objects without ego — no signature, no brand, just pure utility becoming art. and i keep thinking about how that's like the total opposite of modern design where everything is about the designer's personal brand

That's such a sharp observation. There's a real tension between the mingei philosophy of ego-less creation and the contemporary design world where your Instagram grid IS your portfolio. Yanagi would probably have some strong feelings about Dribbble.

He was also really influenced by seeing Korean and Okinawan ceramics, right? The idea that beauty wasn't something you consciously imposed on an object but something that emerged from repeated, honest making. Do you think that philosophy has any practical application for modern designers, or is it more of a conceptual north star?

both honestly. like on a practical level, mingei is basically why muji exists. the whole muji brand philosophy is literally yanagi's ideas filtered through late 20th century retail — no brand, no excess, let the material speak. kenya hara talks about this all the time, the idea of 'emptiness' as a design strategy. and honestly the typography choices in muji's early catalogs are SO good, just clean neutral faces with incredible spacing

The Muji connection is really well drawn — Kenya Hara essentially took Yanagi's philosophical framework and turned it into a viable commercial design language. "Emptiness" as design strategy rather than minimalism as aesthetic — there's a meaningful difference there. Minimalism can feel like a stylistic choice, but what Hara describes is more about creating space for the user's own meaning.

The typography thing is interesting too — those early Muji catalogs used type almost as negative space itself. Not calling attention to the typographic choices but letting them recede into pure readability. Which is kind of the typographic equivalent of the anonymous craftsman, now that I think about it.

Do you see mingei influence in other contemporary Japanese brands or design movements?

oh for sure. like the whole wabi-sabi aesthetic that western designers keep trying to appropriate is rooted in a lot of the same ideas — imperfection, transience, the beauty of worn surfaces. but most western designers just slap a crackle texture on something and call it wabi-sabi which is like... no. you missed the whole point lol. the point isn't the texture, it's the philosophy that the texture represents. a thing that has been used and worn is beautiful BECAUSE of its use, not because someone applied a photoshop filter

Ha — the "crackle texture Photoshop filter" wabi-sabi is a perfect example of aestheticizing a philosophy without understanding it. It's like putting a serif font on something and calling it "editorial design."

You're getting at something really fundamental about the difference between surface-level style borrowing and actually engaging with the ideas underneath. Yanagi would say that trying to deliberately make something look wabi-sabi defeats the entire purpose — the beauty of a worn tea bowl comes from decades of actual use, not from a designer deciding it should look worn.

There's an interesting parallel to how craft beer branding keeps using "authentic handmade" aesthetics that are all produced digitally with the same three rough script fonts. The signifiers of authenticity have become completely detached from actual authenticity.

Have you been reading Yanagi's original texts, or more contemporary writing about mingei?

i found a translation of 'the unknown craftsman' at a used bookstore in shinsekai last week and it absolutely wrecked me. like yanagi's writing is so clear and unpretentious which is kind of perfectly consistent with his whole thing right? he writes about beauty the way he says beautiful objects should be made — without showing off. the chapter on seeing and knowing is incredible, he argues that intellectual analysis actually prevents you from truly seeing beauty. you have to receive it intuitively

Finding that at a used bookstore in Shinsekai of all places feels poetically appropriate — Yanagi would approve of the object finding its way to you through ordinary commerce rather than you ordering it from Amazon.

"The Unknown Craftsman" is genuinely one of those books that reframes how you think about creative work. The seeing vs. knowing distinction is profound — the idea that the moment you start analyzing why something is beautiful, you've already stepped away from the direct experience of its beauty. There's a real parallel to how designers can over-intellectualize their process to the point where the intuitive eye gets buried under rationale.

The translation by Bernard Leach is the classic one, right? Leach was himself a fascinating bridge figure between Japanese and Western craft traditions.

Do you find that reading stuff like this changes how you approach your own design work, or does it stay more in the 'philosophical inspiration' zone? Like, is there a way to apply the mingei sensibility to commercial design projects, or is there an inherent contradiction there?

honestly i think it's changed how i think more than how i execute, if that makes sense. like i'm still making logos in digital tools for paying clients which is about as far from anonymous folk craft as you can get lol. but it's shifted my hierarchy of what matters — like i find myself caring less about whether something looks clever or impressive and more about whether it actually works, whether it serves the thing it's supposed to serve. which sounds basic but honestly most designers are optimizing for peer approval not user function

That doesn't sound basic at all — that's a genuinely mature shift in design thinking. Moving from "will other designers think this is impressive" to "does this honestly serve its purpose" is harder than it sounds because the entire design community rewards the former. Your portfolio gets attention for cleverness, not for quiet utility.

But the best design work usually IS the quiet work — the packaging that helps someone find the right product instantly, the brand identity that makes a small business feel legitimate and trustworthy, the UI that people don't even notice because it just works. That's very much in the mingei spirit, even if the medium is digital.

Yanagi would probably argue that the contradiction between commercial design and mingei isn't as absolute as it seems — the craftsman making rice bowls was also making functional objects for commerce. The difference was the lack of ego in the making, not the absence of a market.

Sounds like that used bookstore find was well timed for you.

yeah honestly it came at the right moment. i've been thinking a lot about what kind of designer i want to be going forward and reading yanagi is like... recalibrating the compass. anyway thanks for indulging my design history nerd spiral, this was fun

This was a great conversation — your connection between mingei philosophy and the peer-approval trap in modern design is genuinely insightful. Enjoy the rest of "The Unknown Craftsman." If you get through it and want more in that vein, Soetsu Yanagi's "The Beauty of Everyday Things" is a collection of his shorter essays that covers similar ground in more digestible pieces. Happy reading.

oh wait i didn't know about that one, adding it to my list. honestly i could read about this stuff all day which is dangerous because i already have too many books stacked on my desk lol. okay for real going now, thanks again
Another book on the stack is very on-brand. Go enjoy the rabbit hole — and maybe move some of those desk books to a shelf before they become your next monitor stand. Talk later.
